bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame U.S. action tentatively as "plans to" or "considers charge" against "former Cuban president" Raúl Castro, emphasizing "escalation in tensions" and de-emphasizing regime atrocities, while right-leaning portray it assertively as "moving to indict" the "infamous dictator" for a "deadly '90s attack," spotlighting "revolutionary" violence and potential drug charges.

summary: On Thursday, a Justice Department official said prosecutors plan to indict 94-year-old former President Raul Castro, with the Attorney's Office for the Southern District of Florida overseeing the effort.
CBS reported the potential indictment focuses on Cuba's deadly 1996 shootdown of planes operated by humanitarian group Brothers to the Rescue.
President Donald Trump has increased pressure on Cuba, threatening sanctions on fuel suppliers, which has ignited power outages and damaged the island's economy.
Cuban officials confirmed Thursday they met with CIA chief John Ratcliffe, who told them The United States is prepared to engage if Cuba makes "fundamental changes."
Representatives for Cuba's foreign ministry and a Justice Department spokesman did not respond to requests for comment, leaving the planned indictment unconfirmed.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage celebrates the Supreme Court's ruling as "ensuring full access" to mifepristone via telehealth and mail, using emotionally charged terms like "seethe" to deride Alito and Thomas's dissent while highlighting women's access and state challengers like Louisiana officials.
Center-Leaning outlets neutrally frame it as "maintaining widespread access" to the "common abortion pill," de-emphasizing dissents and such restrictions.
Pivotal divides center on negatively portraying conservative justices and prioritizing convenience over safety concerns.
Both perspectives agree on preserving status quo pending litigation, revealing deeper reproductive rights tensions.
summary: On Thursday, May 14, 2026, the Supreme Court preserved access to the abortion drug mifepristone, rejecting lower-court restrictions that would have halted mail-order prescriptions while the legal challenge continues.
The New Orleans-based 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ordered the FDA to change rules for the medication on May 1, responding to Louisiana's lawsuit that blocked mail-order access to the drug.

Danco Laboratories, which manufactures the brand-name version Mifeprex, warned the ruling would be "direct, immediate, and chaotic," preventing patients from picking up prescriptions even in-person at pharmacies.

Louisiana Attorney General Liz Murrill argued that "hundreds of abortions are occurring every month" in the state despite bans, while Justices Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito dissented from the emergency order.
Access will likely remain uninterrupted until next year as the case proceeds, though the FDA is currently reviewing safety protocols that could eventually overturn mail-order availability.
title: Supreme Court allows mail-order of abortion pill mifepristone pending appeal

Right-Leaning coverage counters by highlighting Cuba "runs out of oil" in its "dictatorship" and "failed nation" status, emphasizing US leverage and Trump's demands for reform.

All agree on the meeting's occurrence amid tensions, underscoring ideological clashes on sanctions versus socialist legitimacy.
summary: On Thursday, CIA Director John Ratcliffe visited Havana, Cuba to meet with Interior Ministry counterparts and foster political dialogue between the long-strained adversaries.
President Donald Trump initiated this dialogue while maintaining pressure on Cuba through a fuel blockade that has severely limited the island's power generation capability.
Cuban representatives asserted during the meeting that Cuba poses no threat to United States national security, presenting official evidence to support their position.
While both nations expressed mutual interest in enhancing security cooperation, a lack of specific details regarding Cuba's future participation remained evident after the discussions.
Protests erupted in Havana following prolonged power outages caused by the blockade, raising concerns over worsening conditions for Cuban citizens amid continued economic pressure.
title: CIA director meets with top officials in Havana, Cuban government says

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame senators' unanimous resolution to withhold pay during shutdowns as "shared sacrifice" and proactive unity against dysfunction, using laudatory terms like "unanimously approve" to emphasize collective restraint.

Right-Leaning coverage, conversely, celebrates it triumphantly with exclamations like "Finally!" and phrases such as "forces senators" or "no paychecks," spotlighting Republican Sen. John Kennedy's role in imposing accountability on elites.
Pivotal divides emerge over Kennedy's agency and bipartisan leaders' backing, which right highlights while left de-emphasizes; center coverage neutrally describes "pay freeze" outcomes.
All agree on the post-midterm measure's goal to deter shutdowns, reflecting divides between populist fiscal discipline and anti-partisan harmony.
summary: On Thursday, Senators unanimously approved a resolution to withhold their pay during future government shutdowns. The measure, sponsored by Senator John Kennedy, R-La., requires the Secretary of the Senate to hold compensation until funding is restored.

Record-Breaking impasses over the past 16 months prompted the move, as two recent shutdowns—a 43-day total government lapse and a 44-day Department of Homeland Security closure—created significant financial hardship for federal workers.
Kennedy stated the bill ensures "shared sacrifice" during shutdowns, and the resolution takes effect after the November 3 general election without requiring House approval or presidential signature.

Senator Lindsey Graham previously proposed a constitutional amendment to mandate pay forfeiture, calling it "constitutionally sound," though he found the process too laborious. Kennedy declined to extend the measure to the House, citing tensions between chambers.
Senators will receive full back pay once funding is restored, aligning the Senate with standard treatment of federal workers who typically receive compensation after shutdowns end.
title: Senators approve withholding their own pay during government shutdowns

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the Border Patrol chief's exit as scandal-ridden chaos, spotlighting "abruptly quits" and "sex tourism scandal" to underscore personal misconduct amid immigration crackdowns, while right-leaning coverage portrays it as strategic reform, touting "cleans house," "shakeup continues," and "historic tenure" for transforming a "chaotic border" into the "most secure ever.

Pivotal divides emerge over sex worker allegations—amplified leftward, de-emphasized rightward—and border successes like dramatic crossing drops, with the 37-year veteran hailed rightward but critiqued leftward.
Center-Leaning reports neutrally note "resigns effective immediately," sidestepping partisan spins.
All agree on the immediate resignation and DHS leadership flux, reflecting deeper immigration enforcement divides.
summary: On Thursday, May 14, 2026, U.S. Border Patrol Chief Michael Banks announced his immediate resignation, marking the latest high-profile departure from the Department of Homeland Security’s leadership team.

Banks first revealed his decision during a Fox News interview, stating, "It’s just time." He claimed that under his 16-month tenure, he successfully steered the agency from a state of "chaotic" insecurity to overseeing the most secure border in U.S. history.

While Banks cited a desire to return to his family ranch in Texas, his resignation comes amid scrutiny over federal immigration operations in Democratic-led "sanctuary cities," which recently resulted in the fatal shooting of two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis by federal agents.

Banks is the third major immigration official to exit since March, following the ouster of former DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and the retirement of commander Gregory Bovino. He is being succeeded on an interim basis by a veteran agency official as the administration "recalibrates" its enforcement strategy.
title: U.S. Border Patrol Chief Mike Banks resigns

summary: On Friday, U.S. President Donald Trump concluded a three-day Beijing summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, claiming progress in stabilizing relations despite deep disagreements on key global issues.
Trump touted "fantastic trade deals" including 200 Boeing jets, but financial markets remained skeptical, noting the order fell short of the roughly 500 expected by investors.
While agreeing the Strait of Hormuz must remain open to support global energy flow, Xi warned that mishandling Taiwan could lead to "clashes and even conflicts" between the superpowers.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio told NBC News that U.S. policy toward Taiwan remains "unchanged," framing Xi's warnings as standard diplomatic practice rather than new escalation.
Trump invited Xi to visit the White House on September 24 for follow-up discussions, though China has yet to publicly confirm whether the president will accept the invitation.
title: Trump departs China after 2-day summit with Xi, lauds progress in ties

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Iran's military as "no longer a threat to neighbours," highlighting "dramatically degraded" capabilities in a dovish tone promoting de-escalation, while right-leaning ones celebrate U.S. dominance with "destroyed...for a full generation," "90 Percent...Gone," and "Epic Fury," employing triumphant rhetoric.

Pivotal divides arise over proxy cutoffs to Hamas and Hezbollah—emphasized rightward, de-emphasized leftward—and retained "very moderate" strike ability, which center coverage qualifies as "diminished but not eliminated" amid "threats remain.
All concur on Admiral Cooper's testimony of 90% defense setbacks, revealing ideological rifts between hawkish prowess and diplomatic relief.
summary: Admiral Brad Cooper, head of US Central Command, told a US Senate committee on Thursday that military actions reduced Iran's threat, with the country's defense industry set back 90 per cent.
Cooper said Iran can no longer transfer arms to allies including Hezbollah in Lebanon, Houthis in Yemen, and Hamas in Gaza, declaring "those transfer paths and methods have been cut off."
On Thursday, maritime security agency UKMTO reported "unauthorised personnel" seized a ship near Fujairah, while an Indian cargo vessel was sunk off Oman with all 14 crew members rescued.
Separately, an Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps commander said 30 ships passed through the Strait of Hormuz since Wednesday night, stressing vessels linked to "an enemy state" remain barred.
Security remains sensitive as Fujairah serves as the UAE's sole oil port outside the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran recently released an expanded map claiming control over that coastal area.
title: CENTCOM Chief Tells Senators Iran's Hold on Strait of Hormuz Has Weakened, but Threats Remain

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the discovery with grandiose terms like "behemoth" and "colossus," emphasizing peer-reviewed publication and its shake-up of the scientific community, while right-leaning coverage highlights visceral scale via "twice the size of a Tyrannosaurus rex" and "9 elephants" equivalence.
Left employs awe-inspiring rhetoric for academic prestige; right opts for populist, relatable hype.
Pivotal divides include the T-rex analogy, de-emphasized by left, and ecosystem details.
Center adds researcher affiliations.
All converge on the 27-30 meter, 27-ton sauropod as Southeast Asia 's largest, uniting awe across interpretive styles.
summary: On Thursday, researchers announced the discovery of Nagatitan chaiyaphumensis, a giant plant-eating sauropod unearthed in Thailand roughly 113 million years ago. This dinosaur is the largest ever found in Southeast Asia, measuring almost 90 feet long.

Rising atmospheric CO2 levels and arid conditions in Early Cretaceous Southeast Asia likely drove the species to evolve such massive proportions. Nagatitan used its long neck and tail to shed heat, thriving in savanna-like environments near meandering rivers.

Lead author Thitiwoot Sethapanichsakul described the specimen as a somphospondylan sauropod belonging to the Euhelopodidae family, unique to Asia. Researchers estimated the dinosaur weighed around 27 tons, with its front leg bone measuring around 5.8 feet long.

The study, published in Scientific Reports, dubs Nagatitan the 'last titan' of Thailand because the region later transformed into a shallow sea. It marks the 14th dinosaur species named in the country.

Excavation remains ongoing at the Chaiyaphum Province site, where experts believe additional fossils exist. Sethapanichsakul noted that researchers recently identified other large sauropod remains in a different Thai province, suggesting more discoveries may follow.
title: Giant new dinosaur identified from fossils in Thailand

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the House's 212-212 tied rejection—its third failure—as a narrow missed chance to "rein in" Trump's aggressive Iran powers, employing skeptical tones with phrases like "rein in" to evoke unchecked hawkishness.

Right-Leaning outlets counter by highlighting Democrats' "fail to fracture" GOP unity behind "Trump's Iran strategy," using triumphant language like "blocks" to celebrate preserved executive strength.
Pivotal divides emerge over constitutional war powers debates, emphasized rightward, versus leftward focus on halting a "military campaign.
Center coverage notes "GOP rebels" more dramatically.
All agree on the Democratic-led tie's narrowness, underscoring ideological rifts on presidential authority versus congressional checks.
summary: On Thursday, the House of Representatives deadlocked 212-212 on a War Powers Resolution aimed at ending President Donald Trump 's Iran military campaign, marking the third failed attempt this year to restrict the conflict.

After the 60-day statutory deadline expired May 1, the administration claimed hostilities terminated following a ceasefire, yet the United States military continues enforcing a naval blockade of Iranian ports.

Three Republicans—Thomas Massie of Kentucky, Brian Fitzpatrick of Pennsylvania, and Tom Barrett of Michigan—broke with their party to support the measure, while Representative Jared Golden of Maine was the lone Democrat to vote against it.

The House deadlock follows a narrow Senate vote on Wednesday, where a similar resolution failed 49-50 as Senators Rand Paul of Kentucky, Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, and Susan Collins of Maine joined Democrats in advancing the effort.

Amid reports of 13 American deaths and at least 405 wounded, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told Senator Lisa Murkowski on Tuesday that Trump already possesses the authority to continue operations without seeking formal congressional authorization.
title: House Republicans narrowly block effort to end Trump’s war with Iran

summary: On Thursday, the Trump administration announced $1.8 billion in new humanitarian aid for the United Nations , framing the allocation as a push for greater efficiency and transparency in U.S. contributions.

This allocation follows a broader 'humanitarian reset' the administration initiated, criticizing the organization for 'ideological creep' and 'bureaucratic inefficiencies' while cutting overall foreign aid billions.
Jeremy Lewin, a Department of Government Efficiency official, stated 92% of this assistance is 'hyper-prioritized' to align with U.S. foreign policy interests rather than U.N. priorities.

In a Thursday report, Human Rights Watch labeled the ongoing 'retreat in foreign funding' an 'autocrat's dream,' arguing cuts weaken international mechanisms for holding human rights abusers accountable.

Meanwhile, the United States still owes nearly $4 billion to the United Nations, including $2.4 billion for peacekeeping, while humanitarian chief Tom Fletcher warns his agency remains 'overstretched, underresourced and literally under attack.
title: US pledges $1.8 billion more for UN humanitarian aid even as it’s cut foreign assistance overall

Right-Leaning coverage counters with militaristic tones like "Feds," "CJNG Hit," "failed heir," and "hardened the coup," touting U.S. strikes on cartel hierarchy.

summary: On Thursday, a U.S. federal grand jury expanded charges against Audias Flores Silva, alias 'The Gardener,' second-in-command of Mexico's Jalisco New Generation Cartel, accusing him of methamphetamine trafficking and conspiracy to launder money.

Mexican Navy special forces arrested Flores Silva on April 27 in Nayarit based on information from U.S. agencies. He served as head of security and oversaw drug operations in Jalisco, Zacatecas, and neighboring states.
Authorities identified Flores Silva as a possible successor to leader Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, alias 'El Mencho,' who was killed in February during an army operation in Jalisco.
If convicted, Flores Silva could face between 10 years and life in prison. He also faces prior charges from an August 2020 indictment for trafficking cocaine and heroin.
Designated a terrorist organization by the U.S. in February, the CJNG remains Mexico's largest criminal group with operations spanning 21 of the country's 32 states.
title: US grand jury widens charges against Mexico’s Jalisco New Generation Cartel second-in-command

summary: Rep. Max Miller filed a defamation lawsuit Wednesday in Cuyahoga County against his ex-wife, Emily Moreno, and her legal team, Andrew Zashin and Zashin Law, alleging they spread false abuse claims to destroy his reputation.
The action follows reports alleging Miller 'hurled' boiling water at Moreno, which he denies, amid a contentious custody battle involving their child.

Seeking at least $25,000 in damages, Miller claims the allegations cost him 'lost business opportunities' and 'reduced campaign contributions,' with outlets reaching 'circulation measured in the tens of millions.'
Moreno's spokesperson, Stefan Mychajliw, rejected the lawsuit Thursday, asserting Miller is using the 'same playbook' as in a 2021 case involving former White House press secretary Stephanie Grisham.

Democrat Brian Poindexter is challenging Miller for Ohio 's 7th Congressional District, while Miller's spokesperson noted Children and Family Services previously deemed earlier abuse allegations unsubstantiated.
title: US Rep. Max Miller sues his ex-wife for defamation in escalation of long-running divorce feud

summary: On Wednesday, Sen. John Fetterman accused the Democratic Party of becoming "increasingly anti-American" amid calls against U.S. military intervention in Venezuela, Iran, and Cuba.
Fetterman insisted his views supporting gay rights since 2013 have remained consistent, arguing the Democratic Party shifted focus away from economic priorities he championed.
Noting a shift since the New Deal era, Fetterman said traditional union members have left the Democratic Party to support President Donald Trump, creating a serious realignment.
Fetterman feels "lonely" within the Democratic Party due to his support for Israel and border security, calling colleagues' opposition to rapid AI development "lunacy."
Rejecting genocide claims in Gaza that have killed over 70,000 Palestinians, Fetterman labeled Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene "crazy pants" for her assertions about Israel.
title: Fetterman: Democratic base becoming 'increasingly anti-American'

Right-Leaning coverage counters by highlighting the same phrase as a crisis from left-wing unrest, presenting the policy as prudent for retaining experienced officers amid dangers.
Pivotal divides emerge in threat attributions and recruitment emphasis, with left amplifying "mounting" urgency and right de-emphasizing it for pro-enforcement tones.

All agree on rising threats, retention woes, and bipartisan action, underscoring divides over violence sources in polarized security debates.
summary: The Senate unanimously passed legislation on Thursday allowing Capitol Police officers to apply to extend their service until age 62, raising the current age limit from 60.

Chief Michael Sullivan told Congress that personnel shortages span all operational units, with 300 officers currently eligible for retirement. The department employs around 1,250 uniformed officers but needs 150 more to staff posts without overtime.

Lawmakers faced nearly 15,000 investigated threats in 2025, a 58 percent increase from 2024. This surge in security risks requires retaining veteran officers with institutional memory to protect members.

California Sen. Alex Padilla and Senate Rules Committee Chairman Mitch McConnell, R-Ky., authored the bipartisan bill, with Padilla stating officers have "a tremendous amount of institutional memory, experience and expertise."
The House previously passed a bill allowing officers to serve until age 65. Republican Rep. Bryan Steil of Wisconsin said, "No officer should be forced to retire when they can still do the job.
title: Congress moves to raise retirement age for Capitol Police as threats against lawmakers mount

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the EPA 's proposal as the Trump administration "relaxing limits" on "toxic wastewater," evoking ecological peril from heavy metals in streams, while right-leaning sources portray it as rolling back "Biden standards" to "slash power costs" up to $1.1 billion and "stabilize the national power grid.
Pivotal divides emerge on rule attribution—a three-year-old Biden measure—and de-emphasized economic benefits versus environmental risks.
Center coverage highlights the rule as "unduly costly" amid spiking energy demand.
Amid clashes over affordability versus safeguards, all agree on cost-related EPA rationales, underscoring ideological rifts on deregulation and reliability.
summary: On Thursday, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency announced a proposal to overhaul wastewater limits for steam electric power plants, aiming to stabilize the national power grid and cut energy bills.

EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin argued that the 2024 Biden administration rule "jeopardized many affordable and reliable baseload power plants" while imposing requirements Scott Corwin, President of the American Public Power Association, called "administratively unworkable."

The EPA plans to "rescind certain one-size-fits-all limits" in favor of case-by-case discharge standards, a revision officials estimate could shave up to $1.1 billion off annual electricity generation costs.

Environmental group Earthjustice slammed the proposal as a public health danger, with attorney Thom Cmar warning it would exempt contaminated groundwater from treatment and threaten drinking water sources.
The EPA is now entering a 30-day public comment period before finalizing the rule, which seeks to bolster the coal fleet amid surging electricity demand from artificial intelligence data centers.
title: Trump Administration Aims to Relax Limits on Toxic Wastewater From Coal-Fired Power Plants

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Trump's "cuts" to USAID as sparking a "surge in violence" with "dramatic consequences" and irreversible harm, using causal phrasing like "followed" to heighten emotional urgency, while right-leaning coverage de-emphasizes direct blame, truncating the study's temporal link without typical skepticism toward adversarial research.
Center-Leaning reports differ by highlighting authors' non-blame stance.

summary: On Thursday, a study published in the journal Science linked the dissolution of the U.S. Agency for International Development to increased violence in African regions that previously relied on the agency for support.

President Donald Trump's administration dissolved USAID last year, eliminating more than 90% of foreign aid contracts and cutting some $60 billion in funding that supported African governments responding to multiple crises.

Researchers correlated the abrupt USAID withdrawal with sustained conflict increases in Nigeria, Ethiopia, and Ivory Coast, where programs had helped counter threats from Boko Haram and other insurgent groups.

The Armed Conflict Location & Event Data reported Wednesday that jihadis have increasingly targeted civilians over four years, with senior Africa analyst Ladd Serwat noting lost aid programs removed critical community support.
title: A surge in violence followed Trump's cuts to USAID programs in Africa, a study finds

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame redistricting as a "voting rights fight" eroding "majority-Black districts," amplifying "fiery" testimony decrying MAGA as "the last breath of the Confederacy," while right-leaning ones portray it as triumphantly "wiping out" Democrat seats to "strengthen Republican control" post-Supreme Court ruling.
Left employs sympathetic, historically charged tones evoking racial dilution; right uses aggressive, victorious rhetoric like "favoring Republicans.
Pivotal divides include emotional opposition voices versus race-based districting limits , with the shift from two to one majority-Black district and 5-1 GOP edge touted as partisan wins or losses.

All agree on the 27-10 Senate passage advancing the map, underscoring divides over representation versus legal equity.
summary: On Thursday, the Louisiana Senate voted 27-10 to pass Senate Bill 121, reducing the state's majority-Black U.S. House districts from two to one and potentially securing a 5-1 Republican delegation advantage.

The proposal follows an April 29 U.S. Supreme Court ruling that struck down Louisiana's previous map for over-reliance on race, mandating state lawmakers to redraw districts to comply with judicial standards.

Sen. Jay Morris, the bill's sponsor, admitted the map aims "to maximize a Republican advantage," while Democratic State Sen. Sam Jenkins argued it represents "using partisanship as cover for discriminatory practices."
Gov. Jeff Landry postponed the U.S. House primaries to November 3 to accommodate the new map, with candidate qualifying scheduled for August 5-7.

Across the South, Republican-led states are capitalizing on a weakened Voting Rights Act to redraw maps, with leaders in South Carolina similarly calling special sessions to pursue new congressional districts.
title: Louisiana senators pass new US House map while South Carolina plans for extra redistricting work

summary: China views the U.S. as declining and credits policies implemented during the Trump administration for strengthening China's position, according to a Beijing think tank and researchers at the Brookings Institution.
President Trump claimed that Xi Jinping's remark about the U.S. being a 'declining nation' referred specifically to the Biden administration 's four years, not Trump's tenure.
Chinese officials warned that the Taiwan issue is critical to China-U.S. relations and that mishandling it could lead to clashes or conflicts, raising tensions in diplomatic ties.
Trump expressed optimism about improving U.S.-China relations during the 2026 Beijing summit despite sharp disagreements over Taiwan and regional security.
title: Trump Claims Xi's 'Declining Nation' Remark Targeted Biden

summary: On Sunday, 65-year-old Sheri Mitchell-Clutts shot and killed her 69-year-old husband, Timothy Clutts, at their Russellville, Alabama home, and was arrested and charged with murder.

Initially, Mitchell-Clutts claimed to 911 dispatchers that she shot her husband in self-defense, but she later admitted to investigators that she killed him out of annoyance while recovering from recent open-heart surgery.

Franklin County Sheriff Shannon Oliver reported the suspect actively searched for her husband before firing the fatal shot, while investigators found no domestic-related calls in six years of records.
Mitchell-Clutts is being held without bond at the Franklin County Jail, and officials continue investigating the couple's history to determine what led to the shooting.
The case serves as a reminder of domestic volatility, with some community members questioning how annoyance could motivate such violence as authorities review the couple's past.
title: Woman Fatally Shot Husband Because She Was Annoyed With Him for Checking On Her After Heart Surgery

Right employs accusatory tones stressing vigilance against Iran, contrasting left's detached restraint; center adds drama with "betraying the US" and "nefarious activities.

summary: On Thursday, the FBI announced a $200,000 reward for information leading to the arrest of Monica Witt, a former Air Force intelligence specialist charged with espionage for Iran.

A former counterintelligence officer for the Air Force Office of Special Investigations, Witt defected to Iran in 2013 and was charged in 2019 with providing sensitive National Defense Information to the regime.

Prosecutors alleged Witt revealed a "highly classified intelligence collection program" and the identity of a US intelligence officer to Tehran; Iranian officials provided her "goods and services, including housing and computer equipment."
Special Agent Daniel Wierzbicki of the FBI Washington Field Office stated the agency believes Witt "likely continues to support" Iranian activities while remaining at large.

The FBI warned Witt's information could empower the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, which has elements responsible for intelligence collection and support for terrorist organizations targeting United States interests.
title: FBI Offers $200,000 for Info on Ex-Air Force Officer Charged with Spying for Iran

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Governor Abbott's proposals as aggressive "targeting" of progressive district attorneys like José Garza, using casual "floats" and amplifying "political stunt" claims to evoke skepticism and overreach, while right-leaning coverage portrays them as a decisive "public safety package" "unveiling" accountability for "rogue" prosecutors and repeat offenders, prioritizing victim protection with triumphant tones.
Pivotal divides emerge over Garza's leniency critiques—left highlights as vendettas, right de-emphasizes for broader law-and-order wins.

All agree on the four-point plan's core elements, underscoring ideological rifts in criminal justice reform.
summary: On Thursday, Texas Gov. Greg Abbott unveiled four public safety initiatives for the 2027 Texas Legislature, including creating a statewide prosecutor role and making county district attorneys eligible for impeachment.

Speaking at the Austin Police Association headquarters, Abbott framed the agenda as a direct response to crime victims who feel the current criminal justice system has failed them, specifically targeting Democratic district attorneys.

Under the plan, the governor would appoint a 'professional prosecutor' subject to Senate confirmation to pursue cases local DAs decline. District attorneys are currently the only elected officials not subject to impeachment.

Travis County District Attorney Jose Garza denounced the proposal as a 'distraction' from the governor's "litany of failures." Garza asserted his office has prioritized victim protection and accountability, not governor politics .

Additionally, Abbott intends to expand the Texas Repeat Offender Taskforce to Dallas-Fort Worth, San Antonio, and Austin, and push legislation denying bail for illegal immigrants arrested for felonies during next year's session.
title: Texas Gov. Abbott wants a statewide prosecutor, DA impeachment process

summary: A small medical plane crashed outside Ruidoso, New Mexico, on Thursday morning, killing all four people aboard, according to officials; the aircraft departed from Roswell Air Center headed for Sierra Blanca Regional Airport.
Responding crews navigate difficult terrain in the Capitan Mountains to reach the crash site, Lincoln County Manager Jason Burns said, with the fire estimated to be less than 5 acres.
Conditions across southern New Mexico remain hot and dry, with a red flag fire risk warning issued for the Ruidoso area due to low humidity and wind gusts reaching 35 mph.
The Federal Aviation Administration and the National Transportation Safety Board will investigate the crash, while the U.S. Forest Service works with local agencies to suppress the fire.
Officials continue to evaluate the wreckage of the plane, which was owned by Angel's Envy, to determine if the fire is directly related to the accident since the cause remains unknown.
title: Small medical plane crashes in New Mexico mountains, killing all 4 people aboard

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump's sanctions as aggressive "targeted" attacks and a "push to punish" "prestigious" firms, highlighting phrases like "strike at the heart of the rule of law " to evoke institutional victimhood, while right-leaning outlets de-emphasize such drama, neutrally noting "sanctions" with minimal context.
Pivotal divides emerge on court skepticism and presidential overreach, with left portraying judicial pushback heroically and right sidelining it.
Center coverage details four elite firms and four judges' unconstitutional rulings, adding specificity absent elsewhere.
All agree on the appeals hearing and prior blocks, underscoring divides over executive power versus legal protections.
summary: On Thursday, a three-judge panel of the District of Columbia Circuit Court heard arguments challenging President Donald Trump's executive orders targeting four major law firms.

Trump sanctioned WilmerHale, Jenner and Block, Perkins Coie, and Susman Godfrey for employing lawyers associated with the Russia probe or the Democratic National Committee, revoking security clearances and barring federal building access.

Deputy Associate Attorney General Abhishek Kambli argued the administration maintains authority over security clearances, while Solicitor General Paul Clement contended the orders violate the First Amendment by punishing lawyers for zealous representation.
Although lower courts previously blocked enforcement of these executive orders, the appellate panel did not immediately issue a ruling following Thursday's two-hour hearing.
title: Law firms urge appeals court to keep blocking Trump’s sanctions against them

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Trump's Xi talks through confrontational lenses like "annihilation" for Iran, emphasizing aggressive ultimatums amid ongoing Chinese oil purchases, while right-leaning ones celebrate diplomatic triumphs with phrases like "major win," "lost their life line," and "leverage belongs to Trump," highlighting Xi's "committed" stance as authoritative revelation amid Hormuz "rattle.
Pivotal divides emerge on right-exclusive allegations of China 's prior dual-use tech aid, triggering hawkish reversals versus left-de-emphasized victories.
Center coverage neutrally relays "vowed" offers on Hormuz without partisan flair.
All agree on Xi's no-military-equipment pledge and open straits, underscoring ideological rifts in assessing Trump's geopolitical clout.
summary: On Friday, President Donald Trump concluded his three-day trip to China after talks with Chinese President Xi Jinping, declaring Iran must either negotiate a deal or face "annihilation."
While China buys nearly 90 per cent of oil from Iran, Xi offered to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz, telling Trump: "If I can be of any help whatsoever, I would like to help."

Trump suggested China agreed to order 200 Boeing jets, though shares fell more than 4% following the comments; separately, Joint Chiefs of Staff Chairman General Dan Caine claimed the US could neutralize Kharg Island in four or five minutes.

During an opulent banquet in the Great Hall of the People, American business figures including Elon Musk and Apple's Tim Cook joined the leaders, as Trump praised Xi as a "warm person" who is "all business."

Highlighting his personal rapport with Xi, Trump stated, "We do great with China," while confirming Beijing would continue purchasing oil from Iran, which the President indicated he did not challenge during the summit.
title: Trump: China has committed to withholding military equipment from Iran

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the war as intensifying Lebanon's "economic crisis" via "price gouging" and "job losses," deploying loaded terms implying business exploitation and emotionally charged anecdotes of "Israeli strikes" demolishing stores and buildings to underscore civilian plight.
Right-Leaning outlets de-emphasize these strikes and personal stories, presenting woes like "slow business" more neutrally without attributing blame.
Center coverage includes strike details but neutrally, avoiding advocacy.
Pivotal divides: military actions' human toll.
All concur war triggers regional shocks, revealing ideological rifts over conflict origins and economic culpability.
summary: Ayman al-Zain saw his sports clothing store destroyed in Israeli strikes on Hezbollah targets.
Ayman al-Zain said prices for store supplies like mannequins and hangers have become very expensive compared to before the war .
Economy Minister Amer Bisat called the war a major economic shock causing business closures, job losses, and fewer tourists, estimating about a 7% loss of GDP.
Bisat's ministry has inspected over 4,000 private businesses and filed many complaints to courts regarding economic issues since the war began.
title: War worsens Lebanon's economic crisis with job losses, price gouging and slow business

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the soldier's death as a neutral "combat" casualty, the 19th in Lebanon, de-emphasizing Hezbollah while noting his hometown for personalization, whereas right-leaning outlets portray a heroic "fall" or "KIA" by "Hezbollah terrorists" via anti-tank missile, touting the 20th fatality and posthumous promotion to evoke military honor.

Pivotal divides emerge in Hezbollah attribution—frequent and vilified on the right, omitted on the left—and weapon specifics, with right using charged terms like "terrorists" for hawkish defiance versus left's restrained tone.
Center personalizes like the right but omits promotions.
All agree on the 20-year-old Golani soldier's death near the Litani River by mortar fire, underscoring divides in militaristic reverence versus contextual caution amid Israel-Hezbollah tensions.
summary: Staff Sergeant Negev Dagan, 20, of the Golani Brigade's 12th Battalion, was killed in southern Lebanon on Thursday night.
Hezbollah terrorists fired an anti-tank guided missile near Dagan's unit operating near the Litani River at approximately 10 p.m., Army Radio's Doron Kadosh reported.
Combat medics attempted to provide medical treatment at the scene but were forced to declare Dagan dead after their efforts proved unsuccessful.
A civilian contractor was also killed in the incident, and the IDF posthumously promoted Dagan from Sergeant to Staff Sergeant.
Dagan is the sixth IDF soldier killed in southern Lebanon since the ceasefire and the nineteenth since hostilities escalated amid the Iran war.
title: Staff-Sgt. Negev Dagan Killed by Hezbollah Mortar Fire Near Litani River in Southern Lebanon

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize aggressive destruction via "bulldozers raze" Palestinian shops for "settlement-linked" roads, highlighting Bedouin displacement and segregation plans to evoke victimhood, while right-leaning frames demolish industrial establishments amid "illegal settlement expansion," using "destroy" to critique expansionism and de-emphasizing unpermitted builds.
Left employs vivid, emotive imagery for outrage; right applies partisan labels like "illegal" for pointed condemnation.
Pivotal divides: humanitarian fallout versus economic impacts of 40-50 shops.
Center notes "tore down" structures without permits, adding rationale.
Both concur on al-Eizariya demolitions for settlement roads, underscoring ideological clashes over occupation.
summary: On Tuesday, Israeli bulldozers demolished dozens of Palestinian shops in Al-Eizariya, clearing land for a road project in the occupied West Bank.

COGAT stated the structures were built illegally and obstructed a planned road, though the Palestinian Authority argues the demolitions facilitate a broader plan to reroute traffic away from Israeli settlements.

Shop owner Mohammad Abu Ghalieh said, "Forty-eight years of night and day to build something for his children and himself, and in one day and one night, everything was gone." Daoud Jahalin reported more than 200 families would lose their incomes.
Attorneys appealed to the Supreme Court, but the demolitions proceeded; Peace Now director Hagit Ofran said the project aims to "divert all Palestinian traffic" to isolate the E1 area.

Critics warn the E1 project isolates Ramallah and Bethlehem, hindering north-south movement for Palestinians; the strategy creates separate road systems for Israelis and Palestinians, complicating efforts to establish a contiguous Palestinian state.
title: Israeli bulldozers raze about 50 Palestinian shops ahead of settlement-linked road project

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame research declines as "a loss for the nation," spotlighting 500 fewer graduate students to evoke national tragedy, while right-leaning emphasize funds "dries up" from Trump-era policies, highlighting staff layoffs, 8% program cuts, and tax rules as ecosystem impacts urging research support.
Center coverage mixes neutral tones with sensational "craters" and "Trump cuts" attributions.
Pivotal divides emerge on Trump blame—de-emphasized leftward—and operational causes rightward.
All align on 10% drops and Kornbluth warnings, underscoring clashes between institutional protection and fiscal scrutiny.
summary: On Thursday, MIT President Sally Kornbluth announced that the university's research enterprise has shrunk 10% from a year ago amid federal funding challenges and policy changes.
The Trump administration raised endowment taxes to 8% and implemented stricter immigration policies that discourage international students, creating a cascade of funding pressures on the institution.
MIT reported a more than 20% decline in federal research awards and faces a $300 million shortfall, forcing administrators to shutter libraries and reduce admissions across departments.
Graduate enrollment outside of the Sloan School declined close to 20% this year, potentially resulting in 500 fewer graduate students entering the university's research pipeline.

Kornbluth is meeting with leaders in Congress and the Trump administration to advocate for research funding while warning that shrinking the pipeline of basic discovery research threatens future scientific innovation.
title: MIT president says research has shrunk 10 percent in 1 year amid Trump cuts

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Israel 's actions through accusatory terms like "rape of Palestinians," portraying the New York Times report as unyielding truth amid suppression attempts, while right-leaning outlets emphasize skepticism via "hideous and distorted lies," "dog rape," and "alleged" claims, depicting the lawsuit as justified defense against media fabrications.

Left employs inflammatory, victimhood-focused rhetoric with phrases like "doubled down," evoking outrage; right uses delegitimizing tones like "controversial" to stoke indignation at anti-Israel narratives.
Pivotal divides emerge over "dog rape" specifics and columnist attribution, de-emphasized on the left.
Center coverage highlights "distorted" reports, tilting toward Israeli quotes.
All agree on the defamation suit and officials' "hideous" rebuke, underscoring pro-Israel versus pro-Palestinian media divides.
summary: On Thursday, The Israeli Ministry announced that Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Foreign Minister Gideon Saar instructed the initiation of a defamation lawsuit against The New York Times over columnist Nicholas Kristof's report.

Monday's opinion piece by columnist Nicholas Kristof detailed alleged sexual violence in the Israeli-occupied West Bank, citing testimonies from 14 men and women who claimed abuse by Israeli settlers or security forces.

The New York Times issued a statement Wednesday affirming support for Kristof's report, stating his accounts were corroborated by witnesses, family, and lawyers, while documenting at least 16 cases of sexual crimes by settlers and security forces.

Netanyahu wrote on Twitter that the publication "defamed the soldiers of Israel" and perpetuated a "blood libel," while The Foreign Ministry rejected the report as relying on unverified sources tied to Hamas-linked networks.

It remains unclear where the lawsuit will be filed or what damages are sought, though the report follows similar testimonies revealed last month by the West Bank Protection Consortium regarding gender dynamics and displacement.
title: Israel to sue New York Times over Palestinian prisoner rape allegations

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame "outside money" and "shaped by" as elite distortion undermining grassroots democracy, evoking skepticism toward donor interference, while right-leaning coverage mixes neutral event reporting with similar phrasing to spotlight external influences potentially corrupting Democratic processes.
Center-Leaning takes neutrally describe these elements without slant.
Pivotal divides emerge on right-only details like the June 2 date and status as one of few competitive primaries, de-emphasized elsewhere to add rarity context.
All perspectives agree on state lawmakers Zach Wahls and Josh Turek debating Thursday to flip Joni Ernst's open seat, underscoring partisan tensions over campaign finance's role in primaries.
summary: Iowa State Sen. Zach Wahls and Rep. Josh Turek are competing for the Democratic Senate nomination, a race increasingly shaped by outside political organizations.
While Wahls and Turek have raised and spent similar amounts personally, financial intervention from outside groups has altered the contest's final stretch as voters prepare for the primary.
Endorsements from former Senate figures and several sitting senators have bolstered Turek, positioning him as the establishment choice in the competitive primary.

During their first debate last week, both candidates aligned on raising the minimum wage and restoring healthcare access, though they drew contrasts on immigration. Turek asked voters, "What are you going to do for Iowa?"
Facing a challenging path in the general election, the nominee must navigate a state that has become increasingly difficult for the Democratic party to regain ground.
title: Democrats will debate in Iowa US Senate primary shaped by outside money and big-name endorsements

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the DOJ lawsuit as escalating a "feud" with a "blatantly partisan" D.C.

Bar over disciplining Trump lawyers, while right-leaning ones portray it as halting the Bar's "weaponizing" of ethics against "Trump-era" attorneys like Jeffrey Clark for 2020 election challenges, using accusatory tones like "unfairly targeting.
Pivotal divides emerge around Clark's election role and Bar actions as partisan excess versus institutional abuse.
Center coverage neutrally emphasizes procedural halts.
Both agree on the lawsuit seeking to block Clark's disbarment, underscoring divides in views on legal accountability versus executive protection.
summary: On Wednesday, the Justice Department filed a lawsuit against D.C. disciplinary officials seeking to nullify disbarment proceedings against Jeffrey Clark, claiming authorities are "weaponizing" state bar discipline to target federal attorneys.
The investigation stems from Clark's attempts to push Justice Department leaders to issue a letter alleging election "irregularities" in Georgia, a swing state Trump lost to Biden by 11,779 votes.

In July, the Appeals Board on Professional Responsibility recommended disbarring Clark for "serious violations of professional conduct," while the Justice Department argues the proceedings violate the Supremacy Clause and Article II.
Other Trump allies have faced similar sanctions: Rudy Giuliani was disbarred in D.C. and New York, and John Eastman's California law license was suspended on an interim basis.
title: Justice Department Sues DC Authorities over Efforts to Discipline Jeff Clark, Ed Martin
topic_full: Washington DC Politics · District of Columbia

summary: Former New York City Mayor Rudy Giuliani returned to his conservative talk show, "The Rudy Giuliani Show," on Wednesday for the first time since being hospitalized for viral pneumonia earlier this month.
Earlier this month, Giuliani was in critical condition and placed on a ventilator at a hospital in Palm Beach, Florida, before beginning his recovery.
Giuliani said "It feels good to be back" while thanking his family, medical staff, and President Donald Trump, who personally called him during his illness.
Defamation judgments totaling $148 million, won by two Georgia election workers, have forced Giuliani into court-ordered asset compensation agreements to settle his legal debts.
Serving an eight-year tenure as mayor punctuated by attacks on the World Trade Center, the Republican later became an adviser to Trump and a vocal proponent of unproven 2020 election fraud claims.
title: Giuliani Returns to Show After Hospitalization: ‘It Is Very Nice to Be Back’

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame Obama's Colbert appearance as a triumphant "defense" demolishing Trump's "biggest mistake" via jabs at his "war" and needless "killing," touting the Iran deal's success sans missiles and pre-2018 withdrawal; right-leaning ones portray it as "pathetic" and "unthinkable" interference— even an "illegal act"—clashing with Trump's China summit, while citing intelligence backing the deal's uranium removal.

Pivotal divides emerge in left's emotive glorification versus right's vilification of Obama's diplomacy boast, with center quoting "pulled it off without firing a missile" more neutrally amid conflicts.
Both agree on the no-missiles claim and uranium extraction, underscoring ideological rifts over diplomatic restraint versus assertive strength.
summary: Former President Barack Obama defended the 2015 Iran nuclear deal during an interview last month with Stephen Colbert at the Obama Presidential Center in Chicago.
The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action , negotiated during his second term, removed 97% of Iran's enriched uranium while curbing their nuclear program without military intervention.
Obama argued the deal was working, noting that both Israeli and U.S. intelligence services agreed, and insisting diplomacy avoided shutting down the Strait of Hormuz.
President Donald Trump countered by withdrawing from the JCPOA in 2018, branding the agreement 'the worst of all time' while pursuing his own deal.
Iran now maintains a stockpile of uranium enriched to 60%, far closer to 90% weapons-grade levels than the 4% limit the JCPOA established.
title: President Obama on Iran: ‘We pulled it off without firing a missile’

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame data centers as "wildly unpopular" with a bigger "NIMBY problem" than nuclear reactors, emphasizing environmental resource drains and stronger Democratic opposition, while right-leaning ones highlight populist "pushing back" to "block" AI expansion in communities, de-emphasizing eco-concerns.
Left employs hyperbolic negativity like "politically toxic," right confrontational terms like "Not In My Backyard," revealing divides on nuclear preferences versus local control.
Center coverage neutrally states "Americans oppose" without spin.
All align on 70-71% opposition and cross-party NIMBY sentiment, underscoring tensions between progressive tech scrutiny and conservative anti-elite resistance.
summary: Seven in 10 Americans oppose the construction of AI data centers in their local communities, according to Gallup data released Wednesday.
Driven by resource consumption fears, half of opponents cite heavy water and energy use as primary reasons, while 16% raise alarms regarding noise, air, and water pollution.

Democrats show the highest levels of intense resistance at 56%, compared to 39% of Republicans; geographically, opposition is highest in the Midwest at 76%, exceeding the 53% opposed to nuclear plants.
Politicians favoring data centers face significant political risk, as the intensity of opposition suggests incoming grassroots activism and legal challenges in upcoming local and state elections.

Expanding artificial intelligence in the U.S. necessitates building more data centers to support computing power, yet nearly half of respondents stated they are 'strongly opposed,' posing a major hurdle.
title: Gallup poll: 7 in 10 Americans don’t want AI data centers near them

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the 218-203 House passage as a "major win" in "bipartisan fashion" for corn growers, emphasizing cross-party harmony, while right-leaning ones portray it as a "significant victory" for biofuels "despite GOP divide," highlighting deregulation triumphs amid refiner cost concerns de-emphasized elsewhere.
Pivotal divides emerge over Republican splits and oil industry "criticism," with left using upbeat tones like "approves" and right employing resilient phrasing like "passes" to expand access.
Center coverage neutrally stresses "splitting Republicans" and vote breakdowns, linking to gas price relief.
All agree on farm boosts and Senate path, underscoring rural economic versus energy sector tensions.
summary: The US House passed the Nationwide Consumer and Fuel Retailer Choice Act 218-203, allowing year-round sales of E15 gasoline, which contains up to 15% ethanol primarily produced in corn-growing states.
The bill aims to increase corn demand and lower consumer fuel costs by enabling more stable and widespread availability of E15.
Opponents argue that the bill increases costs for small refiners and relies on government mandates rather than market competition.
Republican leadership was divided on the bill, with some representatives supporting it to aid farmers, while others opposed it due to concerns about impacts on refining capacity.
title: US House Passes Bill Allowing Year-Round Sales of E15 Gasoline
